planting.note: Plant seeds or divisions in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic, lean to average, loamy or rocky soil in full sun; Georgia aster requires lean, well-drained soils and full sun — it is naturally found on rocky, acidic, clay or sandy soils of xeric (dry) Piedmont forest openings, roadsides, and clearings. Rich, fertile soils cause lush growth that flops and reduces flowering.

directions: Plant seeds or divisions in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic, lean to average, loamy or rocky soil in full sun; Georgia aster is a rare and beautiful native aster endemic to the southeastern US — restricted to a narrow range in the Piedmont of Georgia, North Carolina, South Carolina, and Alabama, where it grows in xeric, fire-maintained openings in longleaf and mixed-pine upland forest. One of the largest-flowered of the native asters, producing abundant showy clusters of violet-purple to lavender flowers with yellow centers in fall (October–November) — among the latest-blooming of all native asters and an exceptionally valuable late-season nectar source for migrating monarchs and native bees. Listed as a species of conservation concern due to habitat loss from fire suppression and development. An outstanding native pollinator plant for dry, acidic, lean soils in the southeastern Piedmont. Closely related to the New England aster but more heat-tolerant and better adapted to the warm, humid Southeast.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring or fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, dry to average, lean to average, loamy or sandy soil in full sun; frost aster is extremely adaptable and virtually impossible to kill once established — thriving in poor, dry soils of roadsides, old fields, dry woods edges, and disturbed areas. Requires no fertilization and self-sows abundantly.

directions: Direct sow in spring or fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, dry to average, lean to average, loamy or sandy soil in full sun; frost aster is one of the most widespread, adaptable, and ecologically important native asters in North America — covering vast areas of old fields, roadsides, dry woodland edges, and disturbed areas from eastern Canada to Texas and Florida. The common name 'frost aster' refers to the pure white ray flowers (which remain white even as temperatures drop), the late blooming season (October through November, after the first frosts in northern areas), and the feathery, silvery-white seed plumes that linger through winter, creating a frosted effect in the garden. The 1/2-inch white flowers with yellow centers, produced in massive quantities on highly branched stems, are extraordinarily abundant and valuable as late-season nectar for monarch butterflies, native bees, and many other pollinators. One of the most important late-season nectar sources in eastern North America. Self-sows prolifically in lean soils. An essential native plant for dry old-field restorations and natural-style gardens seeking maximum wildlife value.

common_name: Silver Lace Vine (Russian Vine, Mile-a-Minute Vine)

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in spring in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry to average, rocky, sandy or loamy, lean to average soil in full sun; silver lace vine is extremely vigorous — plant ONLY where you intend to allow its aggressive growth, as it can quickly overwhelm structures and neighboring plants. It is listed as invasive in some areas.

directions: Plant container-grown plants in spring in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry to average, rocky, sandy or loamy, lean to average soil in full sun; silver lace vine (Russian vine, or 'mile-a-minute' vine for its extraordinary growth rate — up to 15 feet per year) is one of the fastest-growing hardy vines available, capable of covering an entire structure, fence, or wall in a single growing season with dense, twining stems. In late summer and early autumn it produces masses of tiny white to pinkish-white flowers in enormous, frothy plumes covering the entire vine — creating one of the most spectacular displays of any vine in bloom, resembling a cloud of white foam or lace. Native to central Asia (Tajikistan and Afghanistan). Despite its aggressive growth, the vine dies completely to the ground in cold winters, making management easier in zones 4–6 than in milder climates where it may remain semi-evergreen. Valuable for quickly covering unsightly fences, walls, and structures where nothing else would grow fast enough. Do not plant near delicate or small plants it might overwhelm. Potentially invasive — check local status.

planting.note: Direct sow after soil warms in spring in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry, sandy or loamy, lean soil in full sun; plains lovegrass grows naturally in dry, open grasslands, sandy plains, and rocky desert slopes of the Southern Plains and Southwest. Excellent as an ornamental grass for hot, dry, full-sun locations.

directions: Direct sow after soil warms in spring in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry, sandy or loamy, lean soil in full sun; plains lovegrass is a medium-sized, warm-season native bunchgrass of the Southern Plains and Chihuahuan Desert transition zone, producing delicate, extremely fine-textured, open, and airy panicles of tiny flowers and seeds from mid-summer through fall. The seed panicles create a misty, diaphanous effect — a haze of fine purple-pink to silvery seed heads floating above the grass clumps. The 'lovegrass' common name (shared by many Eragrostis species) references the delicate, romantic quality of the seed heads. Provides important food for doves, sparrows, and other seed-eating birds through fall and winter. Distinguished from the non-native weeping lovegrass (E. curvula) by its native origin, finer texture, and less weeping, more upright form. An excellent native ornamental grass for hot, dry, full-sun gardens and naturalistic prairie plantings in the southern plains states and Southwest.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in spring in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile to average, loamy soil in partial shade; Chinese sweetspire is evergreen in zones 7–10 (losing some leaves in colder parts of its range) and prefers partial shade, tolerating more shade than Virginia sweetspire. More heat and drought tolerant than Virginia sweetspire.

directions: Plant container-grown plants in spring in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile to average, loamy soil in partial shade; Chinese sweetspire is the Asian counterpart of Virginia sweetspire (Itea virginica) — a graceful, slightly larger evergreen shrub from the forests and stream banks of central and southern China, with similar (but somewhat larger and more leathery) glossy green leaves and long, fragrant, white flower spikes in summer. More heat- and drought-tolerant than Virginia sweetspire, and evergreen in zone 7–10 rather than deciduous. The long (4–6 inch), slender, fragrant white flower spikes are produced in summer and have a pleasant, sweet scent. Fall color in mild climates is less spectacular than Virginia sweetspire (which turns brilliant red), remaining more greenish-yellow in warmer zones. A refined, shade-tolerant evergreen shrub for the mild, humid Gulf Coast and similar climates where an elegant partial-shade shrub with fragrant summer flowers is desired. Less commonly grown than Virginia sweetspire but valuable for its additional heat-tolerance and evergreen quality in the South.
